cards.front: List the 7 OSI layers from layer 7 to layer 1.
cards.back: Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical. Top-down mnemonic: 'All People Seem To Need Data Processing'. Bottom-up: 'Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away'.

cards.front: How does the TCP/IP 4-layer model map to OSI?
cards.back: Application (= OSI 5/6/7), Transport (= OSI 4), Internet (= OSI 3), Link / Network Access (= OSI 1/2). The 5-layer hybrid model splits Link into Data Link and Physical and is what most CCNA material uses.

cards.front: What is the PDU name at each TCP/IP layer? (App, Transport, Internet, Link)
cards.back: Application: data/message. Transport: segment (TCP) or datagram (UDP). Internet: packet. Data Link: frame. Physical: bits. Mnemonic: 'Some People Fear Birthdays' (segment, packet, frame, bits, bottom-up).

cards.front: Define encapsulation and de-encapsulation in TCP/IP.
cards.back: Encapsulation = adding a layer's header (and possibly trailer) as data passes down the stack on the sender. De-encapsulation = stripping each header back off as data passes up the stack on the receiver.

cards.front: Which OSI/TCP-IP layer adds a TRAILER (in addition to a header)?
cards.back: Data Link layer (e.g., Ethernet adds an FCS trailer for error detection — 4 bytes of CRC). Other layers only add a header.

cards.front: Same-layer interaction vs. adjacent-layer interaction — what's the difference?
cards.back: Same-layer interaction = two hosts' Layer N peers communicate via the layer-N header (e.g., TCP-to-TCP). Adjacent-layer interaction = on a single host, layer N uses services from layer N-1 to send data.

Modularity (vendors can innovate at one layer without breaking others), troubleshooting (isolate problems by layer), and standardization (common interfaces). Real protocols don't always perfectly fit one layer.

cards.front: Which 3 protocols are the canonical TCP/IP application-layer examples on the CCNA?
cards.back: HTTP (web), DNS (name resolution), DHCP (address assignment). All run over TCP or UDP at the transport layer.
